query: association between deletion at 6q24.2-26 and overall survival in ovarian cancer patients

retrieved_docs.title: Cancer Antigen 125 -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Preoperative and postoperative CA125 concentrations may be of prognostic significance. [41] After primary surgery and chemotherapy, persistent elevations of CA125 concentrations are associated with poor prognosis. Patients with preoperative CA125 concentrations greater than 65 U/mL are reported to have a lower 5-year survival rate and a 6.37-fold risk of death compared to patients with CA125 levels less than 65 U/mL. [21] The half-life of the CA125 antigen is reported to have an additional prognostic value. A half-life of less than 20 days is associated with improved survival compared to a half-life of more than 20 days. [26] Normalization of CA125 levels after 3 cycles of combination therapy also correlates with improved survival. Importantly, CA125 concentration is not elevated in 10% to 20% of patients with advanced ovarian cancer. For these patients, using radiological imaging techniques and monitoring other tumor markers are necessary. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: At the time of diagnosis, the patient should be counseled on their prognosis and all available treatment options. Genetic counselors and oncology clinicians should counsel all patients with ovarian cancer regarding genetic testing, especially for patients with hereditary cancer syndromes. [6] The palliative care team and other related consultants should be promptly involved regardless of cancer stage to enable comprehensive care, anticipate the disease course, and significantly impact the patient's quality of life. Patients should also be advised on ongoing clinical trials if pertinent to their particular case.

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Recurrent Ovarian Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: PARP inhibitors have been under clinical development at various stages and have shown their efficacy in patients with germline BRCA mutations. They were first approved as monotherapy in ovarian cancer patients with deleterious germline or somatic BRCA mutations who have not responded to chemotherapy. Further studies showed significant progression-free survival benefits in patients with an initial response to bevacizumab with maintenance PARP inhibitor therapy. An overall survival benefit is yet to be proven, which requires a longer follow-up. SOLO-2 study assessed maintenance monotherapy with olaparib in patients with platinum-sensitive recurrent ovarian cancer and BRCA mutation, showing significantly improved progression-free survival for the patients receiving olaparib with no significant detrimental effect on the patient's quality of life. [60]

retrieved_docs.title: Wilms Tumor -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis varies by tumor stage and histology. Favorable histology has survival rates of 99% to 86%, while unfavorable histology survival ranges from 84% to 38%, depending on the stage. [18] [19] [20] [21] End-stage renal failure occurs in about 1% of patients, usually due to metachronous bilateral tumors. Females with Wilms tumor-aniridia (WAGR) syndrome may develop streaked ovaries and are at an increased risk for developing gonadoblastoma. A poorer prognosis is associated with the following characteristics: Anaplastic histology in stage II to IV tumors Diffuse anaplasia is worse than focal Loss of heterozygosity at chromosomes 1p, 1q, 11p15, and 16q or presence of TP53 Higher stage (most epithelial predominant tumors are stage I; most blastema predominant tumors are stage III and IV) Age older than two years Higher positive lymph node density Large tumor size Even small tumor foci can be associated with a poorer prognosis due to resistance to chemotherapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Poly(ADP)-ribose polymerase inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: PARP inhibitors have recently gained momentum for the maintenance treatment of ovarian cancer. PARP inhibitor agents include olaparib, niraparib, and rucaparib. Olaparib was the first Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved drug in this subgroup indicated to treat advanced BRCA mutated ovarian cancer after platinum-based chemotherapy, based on SOLO-1, phase 3 randomized double-blind, placebo-controlled trial. Olaparib reduced disease progression or death by 70% (hazard ratio 0.30, 0.23 to 0.41; P < .001). [51] The PAOLA-1 trial, a phase 3 randomized controlled trial of 806 women with stage III to IV high-grade serous or endometrioid ovarian cancer, showed a progression-free survival benefit of 4.5 months in the group that received olaparib and bevacizumab maintenance versus placebo and bevacizumab. [52] This combination of olaparib, niraparib, or rucaparib with bevacizumab achieved FDA and NCCN approval as a first-line maintenance treatment for these patients with ovarian cancer after initial platinum-based chemotherapy with partial or complete response or tumors associated with homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) defined by the presence of deleterious BRCA mutation. Recent clinical trials include the VELIA and PRIMA trials using a newer PARP inhibitor, veliparib, and niraparib maintenance therapy, respectively, showed markedly improved progression-free survival compared to the placebo group in patients with newly diagnosed advanced-stage ovarian cancer who initially responded to first-line platinum-based chemotherapy. [53] [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Endometrial Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Targeted Therapy
retrieved_docs.content: So far, options for molecularly targeted treatment of endometrial cancer are mainly limited to trials. The poly adenosine diphosphate (ADP)-ribose polymerase (PARP) inhibitor olaparib was approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in 2014 for treating progressive ovarian cancer in BRCA1 or BRCA2 germline mutation carriers who have failed at least 3 previous anti-cancer drug regimens. Prospective trials of olaparib's effectiveness against endometrial cancer are ongoing based on published improvements in overall response rates and mean progression-free survival, though not overall survival, in previously treated patients with ovarian cancers associated with germline or somatic BRCA mutations. The greatest benefit of PARP thus far has been prolonging progression-free survival in ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma with BRCA mutations. Olaparib was FDA-approved in 2017 for maintenance therapy of patients with ovarian, fallopian tube, or primary peritoneal cancer in complete or partial response to platinum-based chemotherapy. Another PARP inhibitor, rucaparib, was approved by the FDA in 2016 for the treatment of advanced ovarian cancers associated with germline or somatic BRCA mutations after 2 or more previous chemotherapies. Because PARP inhibition has been demonstrated in PTEN- deficient endometrial cancer cell lines, these agents may be effective in MMRd endometrial cancers with microsatellite instability. Finally, immunotherapy to block the programmed death-1 pathway, which, if left unchecked, represses cytotoxic immune responses, was associated with improved responses in patients with MMRd endometrial cancers that are characterized by marked tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes, which are hallmarks of Lynch syndrome-linked cancers. The FDA approved pembrolizumab for MMRd endometrial cancer treatment, and in 2021, dostarlimab was also approved. [9] [34] [37] [38] [39] [40] [41] [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Ovarian cancer has a generally poor prognosis, with a 5-year survival rate of less than 50% and a 10-year survival rate of approximately 35%. [71] The prognosis of ovarian cancer is directly dependent on the disease stage at the time of diagnosis. The survival rate for stage I ovarian cancer is estimated to be between 70% to 92% compared to less than 6% for stage IV tumors. [72] Other factors associated with prognosis include baseline performance status, histologic type and grade, and volume of residual disease post-primary cytoreductive surgery. Because the amount of residual disease after primary debulking surgery impacts prognosis so significantly, guidelines recommend that this treatment should be performed in any patient able to tolerate surgery. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Carcinogenesis -- Clinicopathologic Correlations -- Mutation
retrieved_docs.content: The TP53 gene, located on chromosome 17p13, is often referred to as the "guardian of the cell" due to its vital role in preventing abnormal cell proliferation. TP53 encodes the P53 protein—one of the most extensively studied proteins in carcinogenesis. The role of P53 in cancer development varies depending on the context, influencing initiation, promotion, or progression. P53 is essential for the expression of genes involved in DNA repair, apoptosis, and cell cycle arrest. In breast and ovarian cancers, especially those classified as basal cancers due to the expression of cytokeratins, the TP53 gene is crucial for initiation. Germline mutations in TP53 are significantly associated with tumor initiation. Li-Fraumeni syndrome, inherited in an autosomal dominant manner, is characterized by a high risk of various cancers, including early-onset breast cancers and sarcomas. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Tissue Evaluation for HER2 Tumor Marker -- Etiology and Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: HER2 amplification or overexpression occurs in approximately 20% to 30% of human breast cancers and some ovarian and gastric cancers. When aberrant, HER2 generates worse biological behavior and clinical aggressiveness in breast cancer. [3] Overexpression of HER2 is primarily attributed to HER2 gene amplification and prompts activation of the HER2 downstream signaling pathway. [4] Breast cancers can possess up to 25 to 50 copies of the HER2 gene and up to a 40- to 100-fold increase in HER2 protein, resulting in 2 million receptors expressed at the tumor cell surface. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Histopathology -- Clear-Cell Ovarian Cancer Histologic Characteristics
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 2.4% of epithelial ovarian cancers are classified as a mucinous subtype. Typically, this subtype has a more favorable prognosis compared to serous ovarian cancers because 80% of mucinous ovarian carcinomas are diagnosed at earlier stages, commonly stage I. Mucinous ovarian carcinomas are often heterogeneous, where a mixture of elements, including benign and malignant tumors, are found in a single specimen. This ovarian cancer subtype is similar to gastrointestinal tract malignancies as complex glandular cytology with architectural features of adenocarcinoma are typically noted on histologic examination. The amount of stromal invasion varies. [1] [26] Because distinguishing primary ovarian mucinous carcinomas from metastatic mucinous appendix tumors is difficult due to their close association, many gynecologic oncologists practice routine appendectomy in patients with ovarian mucinous carcinomas. [27] KRAS mutations are the most frequent molecular alterations identified in mucinous ovarian cancer subtypes. Less frequently, other gene mutations, including HER2 , CDKN2A , and TP53 , are found in molecular studies for this subtype. [1] [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Peutz-Jeghers Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Duodenal cancer is a leading cause of death in individuals with PJS, especially in those with advanced polyposis. Extraintestinal malignancies, including breast, ovarian, and testicular cancers, also contribute to increased mortality risk and require vigilant screening.

retrieved_docs.title: Epithelial Ovarian Cancer -- Treatment / Management -- Surveillance
retrieved_docs.content: Following primary ovarian cancer treatment, continued monitoring for any evidence of recurrence is necessary, though the precise duration and frequency have not been universally established. The NCCN guidelines recommend follow-up visits with a pelvic exam every 2 to 4 months for 2 years, then every 3 to 6 months for 3 years. After the first 5 years, follow-up can be performed annually. Furthermore, a CA-125 level should be assessed in patients with elevated levels before treatment. A complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic profile, and imaging should be performed when clinically indicated. [64] Imaging modalities include chest, abdominal, and pelvic MRI, computed tomography (CT), or positron emission tomography (PET)/CT scan. NCCN guidelines also recommend that a genetic testing referral be offered if it has not already been done. However, some experts recommend routinely imaging and assessing a CA-125 level in patients with stage II to IV ovarian cancers every 3 months the first year, every 4 months the second year, every 6 months in years 3 to 5, and then every year thereafter, instead of just as clinically indicated. Monitoring bone mineral density in patients receiving an aromatase inhibitor is also recommended. [64] [65]

retrieved_docs.title: Granulosa Theca Cell Tumors of the Ovary -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Stage is the most important prognostic factor, followed by tumor rupture, which indicates a worse prognosis. Large size (>15 cm) and bilaterality are unfavorable prognostic factors; however, the prognostic impact of size disappears when corrected for the stage. Mitotic activity or nuclear atypia does not correlate with clinical outcomes. Homozygous FOXL2 mutation, higher FOXL2 mRNA expression, and chromosomal imbalance are associated with early relapse and worse outcomes. [35] [36] Higher levels of SMAD3 expression are a novel predictor of recurrence in early-stage disease. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Peritoneal Cancer -- Pathophysiology -- EOPPC
retrieved_docs.content: Oncogenic stimuli, such as loss of heterozygosity at the p53 or BRCA-1 loci or overexpression of HER-2/neu, contribute to the development of EOPPC. [26] These genetic alterations disrupt normal cellular processes, leading to uncontrolled proliferation and malignant transformation.

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Mesothelioma -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic makeup may influence individual susceptibility to malignant pleural mesothelioma. A loss of 1 copy of chromosome 22 is frequently observed in patients with the disease. Other chromosomal anomalies linked to malignant pleural mesothelioma include deletions in: Chromosomal arm 3p Chromosomal arm 1p Chromosomal arm 6q Chromosomal arm 9p.

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor-Suppressor Genes -- Clinical Significance -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Phosphatase and Tensin Homolog ( PTEN ) Gene: PTEN gene encodes a lipid phosphatase that negatively regulates the phosphoinositide-3-kinase (PI3K)-AKT and the target of mTOR signaling pathways. These pathways are vital for cell proliferation, cell cycle progression, and apoptosis. [24] The PTEN protein also functions to keep migration, adhesion, and angiogenesis in check. It also plays a role in the overall stabilization of the genome. A biallelic loss-of-function is common in diverse cancers. Cowden syndrome is an autosomal dominant disorder resulting from germline loss-of-function mutations of this gene and correlates with a higher risk of breast and endometrial cancer

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2404
retrieved_docs.content: Ovarian cancer, a malignant neoplasm originating in the ovary, is associated with the concept of metastases to the ovary (نقائل إلى المبيض), indicating that secondary tumor spread can involve the ovarian tissue itself. Recognizing that ovarian cancer may include metastatic lesions to the ovary is important for accurate staging, prognosis assessment, and the formulation of appropriate therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2703
retrieved_docs.content: The statement “biopsy of breast showed malignant neoplasm” is an instance that signifies the presence of سرطان الثدي (breast cancer). Breast cancer can extend beyond the breast and may appear as cancer of ovary, which is described as a manifestation of سرطان الثدي. The entity cancer of ovary itself can have a subordinate concept نقائل إلى المبيض (metastasis to the ovary), indicating that ovarian lesions may represent metastatic disease. Thus, a malignant breast biopsy confirms breast cancer, which can metastasize to the ovary, and ovarian involvement may be either a primary ovarian cancer or a secondary manifestation of breast cancer, often accompanied by ovarian metastases. Recognizing these interconnections is essential for accurate staging, prognosis, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2901
retrieved_docs.content: Benign tumor cells are not considered a finding associated with ovarian cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2888
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a diagnostic finding of cancer of the ovary, indicating the presence of abnormal tissue within the ovarian region. Similarly, Tumor cells, malignant are identified as the abnormal cellular component of cancer of the ovary, representing the malignant cells that drive the disease process. Together, these pathological entities underscore the cellular abnormalities that characterize ovarian cancer and are essential for its diagnosis and understanding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2092
retrieved_docs.content: of both ovaries and the uterus (استئصال المبيضين والرحم), reflecting the spectrum of surgical options from organ‑preserving to radical excision. Overall, these interrelated procedures illustrate a structured taxonomy of uterine, cervical, and ovarian surgeries, their co‑occurrence patterns, and their inclusion in a unified classification of malignant‑disease‑related therapeutic and diagnostic interventions, which is essential for accurate coding, clinical decision‑making, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2816
retrieved_docs.content: The Standardized MedDRA Query “أورام الرحم وقناة فالوب الخبيثة غير المحددة (SMQ)” includes the surgical procedure “استئصال البوق والمبيض في جهة اليسار” (left salpingo‑oophorectomy) as a related term, indicating that removal of the left fallopian tube and ovary is a pertinent clinical intervention for patients identified with unspecified malignant uterine or fallopian‑tube tumors. This relationship highlights the direct link between the diagnostic category of malignant uterine and fallopian‑tube neoplasms and the appropriate surgical management aimed at excising the affected reproductive structures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2617
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a pathological finding of ovarian cancer, indicating the presence of cancerous cells within ovarian tissue. Similarly, Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant, are described as abnormal cells of ovarian cancer, reflecting cellular atypia that may be difficult to classify but still signifies disease involvement. Together, these cellular abnormalities underscore key diagnostic features of ovarian cancer and are essential for accurate pathology assessment and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2760
retrieved_docs.content: إزالة المبيض جزئيًا (partial oophorectomy) and استئصال الرحم جزئيًا (partial hysterectomy) are gynecologic surgical procedures that frequently co‑occur, often being performed together in the management of conditions requiring simultaneous partial removal of ovarian and uterine tissue. This co‑occurrence reflects a clinical approach where both organs are addressed in a single operative session to treat disease, reduce patient morbidity, and streamline postoperative recovery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 210
retrieved_docs.content: SMQ for uterine and fallopian‑tube cancers (أورام الرحم وقناة فالوب الخبيثة غير المحددة (SMQ)), indicating that these combined resections are standard therapeutic components in the management of such malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2374
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is identified as a diagnostic finding in both cancer of ovary and السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer), indicating that this histopathologic feature can be present across different organ‑specific malignancies. Likewise, Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant are regarded as abnormal cells of disease in both cancer of ovary and السرطان البنكرياسي, underscoring that indeterminate tumor cell populations may be a common element in the pathology of these cancers. In addition, cancer of ovary has a related subentity described as نقائل إلى المبيض, which denotes metastasis to the ovary. Together, these relationships illustrate that malignant cellular infiltrates and uncertain tumor cells serve as shared pathological markers for ovarian and pancreatic cancers, and that ovarian cancer may involve metastatic spread, information that is valuable for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2734
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is a broad clinical category that includes a range of conditions affecting the female reproductive organs. Within this category, cancer of the uterus represents a malignant neoplasm of the uterine tissue, while ورم مهبلي حميد (benign vaginal tumor) denotes a non‑malignant growth in the vagina. Recognizing both uterine cancer and benign vaginal tumors as specific manifestations of Female Reproductive System Disorder highlights their shared relevance to gynecologic health and underscores the importance of tailored diagnostic and therapeutic strategies for each condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2164
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of ovary is recognized as a distinct malignant disease whose abnormal cellular component is described as “Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant,” indicating that the neoplastic cells in ovarian cancer may initially be difficult to classify but are ultimately considered abnormal cells of the disease. In the diagnostic work‑up, cancer of ovary must be differentiated from سرطان القولون (colon cancer), as both can present with overlapping pelvic or abdominal findings and therefore appear on each other’s differential diagnosis lists. Additionally, cancer of ovary can give rise to or be associated with نقائل إلى المبيض, which denotes metastatic lesions that involve the ovary. Understanding that ovarian cancer may be confused with colon cancer, that its tumor cells are abnormal by nature, and that it can produce ovarian metastases is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2443
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells, benign are regarded as not a finding of disease in both cancer of ovary and السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer), indicating that benign tumor cells do not represent pathological involvement in these malignancies. In contrast, tumor cells, malignant are classified as abnormal cells of disease in both cancer of ovary and السرطان البنكرياسي, reflecting their role as pathologic, disease‑defining elements in these cancers. Similarly, tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant are also considered abnormal cells of disease for cancer of ovary and السرطان البنكرياسي, underscoring that even when the malignant potential is unclear, such cells are treated as disease‑related abnormalities in both ovarian and pancreatic malignancies. This distinction highlights the clinical importance of differentiating benign from malignant or indeterminate tumor cells when evaluating ovarian and pancreatic cancers.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2502
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of ovary is defined by the presence of tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant, which are recognized as abnormal cells of the disease. In addition, cancer of ovary includes the related concept “نقائل إلى المبيض,” indicating metastatic involvement of the ovary. This reflects that ovarian cancer comprises ambiguous tumor cells and may be associated with metastatic spread to the ovary.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2803
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant tumor cells are the abnormal cellular component that defines ovarian cancer, indicating that ovarian cancer is characterized by the presence of these malignant tumor cells.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2881
retrieved_docs.content: Tumor cells that cannot be definitively classified as benign or malignant are considered abnormal cells in the context of ovarian cancer, indicating that such indeterminate tumor cells represent the pathological cellular component of this disease.
